revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:四阿屋神社]]
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|qid=Q812767|1=Shinto}} |name = Azumaya Shrine (四阿屋神社) |image = File:Cinnamomum camphora of Azumaya Shrine in Ushiwaramachi.jpg |location = {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|qid=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture|1=Saga Prefecture}}, [[Tosu City]], Ushiwara-machi |ISO = JP-41 |Latitude = 33.3918528 |Longitude = 130.4793667 |Label Position = bottom |deity = [[Yamato Takeru no Mikoto]] |type = Gōsha (郷社) |consecration_year = Allegedly in the 1st year of Emperor Tenchi's reign (AD {{ill|662|en|662|qid=Q31127|lt=662|1=662}}) |Main Hall = |Festival = April 1 |Rituals = Ota-mai (at Kuranoe Oimatsu Shrine) |Map = Japan Saga |location = {{ill|Tosu|qid=Q860674|1=Tosu, Saga}} |coordinates = {{Coord|33.39185277777778|N|130.47936666666666|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Azumaya Shrine''' (四阿屋神社) is located in Ushiwara-machi, [[Tosu City]], {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|qid=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture|1=Saga Prefecture}}. It served as the '''Sōsha''' (provincial shrine) of the ancient [[Yabu District, Saga|Yabu District]] and was historically referred to as '''Azumaya-gū''' (四阿屋宮). The shrine’s former designation under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was Gōsha (郷社).<ref name="Kadokawa">[[#Kadokawa|Kadokawa Japanese Place name Dictionary]] "Azumaya Shrine" entry. (See: [https://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7215843 JLogos])</ref> == History == Azumaya Shrine is traditionally said to have been established in the 1st year of Emperor Tenchi's reign (AD {{ill|662|en|662|qid=Q31127|lt=662|1=662}}) by enshrining deities from [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|qid=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}}. By AD 791, the shrine also enshrined deities from [[Sumiyoshi Shrine]] and [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]], initially under the name "Azumaya Myōjin."<ref name="Kadokawa"/> According to the ''Saga Prefecture Shrine Records'' (1926), the area had been significant since the time of Emperor Keikō, who stayed in the region during an imperial expedition, and later during Yamato Takeru's conquest of the [[Kumaso]]. The name "Azumaya" refers to a temporary imperial residence in the area, later stylized as "四阿屋" for both the shrine and the surrounding locality.<ref name="Shishiyou">[[#Shishiyou|Saga Prefecture Shrine Records]] (1926), pp. 55–57.</ref> The shrine was referred to as Azumaya Myōjin in the ''[[Ruijū Fusenshō]]'', which records its elevation in AD 920 to the Rank of ''Jūgoige'' (従五位下). By AD 1292, documents from [[Yodohime Shrine]] note that Azumaya Myōjin held control over 3 ''chō'' and 3 ''tan'' of farmland.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> During the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], the shrine suffered significant losses during invasions by [[:en:Ōtomo Yoshishige|Ōtomo Yoshishige]] and {{ill|Shimazu Yoshihisa|en|Shimazu Yoshihisa|qid=Q955727|lt=Shimazu Yoshihisa|1=Shimazu Yoshihisa}}. Despite efforts to rebuild, much of its documents and treasures were destroyed. Its deities were preserved and enshrined in a modest structure.<ref name="Shishiyou"/> In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Azumaya Shrine received patronage from the [[:en:Tsushima Domain|Tsushima Domain]]. Its current structure was rebuilt in AD 1657 under [[Sō Yoshinari]]. The shrine achieved its modern configuration during the [[Hōreki era]] (1760).<ref name="Kadokawa"/> The shrine was designated as a ''Murasha'' (village shrine) in AD 1871 and elevated to Gōsha status in AD 1881. It absorbed the nearby Yamashiro Shrine and Tashiro Shrine during this time.<ref name="Kadokawa"/> == Deities Enshrined == * Yamato Takeru no Mikoto * Sumiyoshi Daimyōjin * Shiga Daimyōjin * Ōyamazumi no Mikoto * Toyouke Ōmikami == Ota-mai (御田舞) == [[File:Oimatsu Shrine in Kuranoue-machi, Tosu main hall.jpg|thumb|200px|Ota-mai performed at Kuranoe Oimatsu Shrine]] The '''Ota-mai of Azumaya Shrine''' (四阿屋神社の御田舞) is a Shinto ritual held in autumn at [[Kuranoe Oimatsu Shrine]] in [[Tosu City]], commemorating agricultural practices to ensure a bountiful harvest. The event is traditionally performed on the third or fourth Sunday of October as part of the ''Okunchi'' festival.<ref name="City1">{{Cite web|title=Ota-mai of Azumaya Shrine|url=https://www.city.tosu.lg.jp/soshiki/26/1761.html|publisher=Tosu City Official Website|date=2021-03-02|accessdate=2024-03-16|qq=}}</ref><ref name="Pref1">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Designated Cultural Properties|url=https://www.pref.saga.lg.jp/kiji0031562/index.html#k_mumi02|publisher=Saga Prefecture|date=2020-06-23|accessdate=2024-03-16|qq=}}</ref> This 40-minute performance features reenactments of traditional rice farming, including plowing, puddling, and planting, culminating in a dramatic ''Oni-mai'' (鬼舞). The ritual is performed by an all-male cast of about 30 members.<ref name="City1"/><ref name="Pref1"/> Ota-mai was designated as an [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] of Saga Prefecture in 1959 and selected as a [[Cultural Property for Preservation|Cultural Property for Documentation and Preservation]] by the national government in 1976.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Ota-mai of Azumaya Shrine|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails/312/543|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|accessdate=2024-03-16|qq=}}</ref> == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Kadokawa Japanese Place name Dictionary Vol. 41: Saga Prefecture|editor=Kadokawa Japanese Place name Dictionary Editorial Board|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=1982|isbn=978-4-04-622957-1|ref=Kadokawa|qq=}} * {{Cite book|title=Saga Prefecture Shrine Records|editor=Michiharu Mitsuhashi et al.|publisher=Saga Prefecture Shinto Priest Association|year=1926|id={{全国書誌番号|43052547|qq=}}|ref=Shishiyou|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|四阿屋神社|version=102729382|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:四阿屋神社]]
